findings.title: No cookie consent banner or mechanism
findings.description: The application uses Plausible Analytics without explicit opt-in consent. GDPR requires freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous consent for non-essential cookies and analytics. The script is loaded unconditionally on every page visit, regardless of user consent status.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Integrate a GDPR-compliant cookie consent library (Cookiebot, OneTrust, tarteaucitron). 2) Implement a consent banner that blocks non-essential scripts until user opts in. 3) Display granular consent choices. 4) Store consent preferences securely. 5) Provide easy withdrawal mechanism.
findings.evidence: <script async src="https://analytics.codyjo.com/js/pa-h94dmAMXaqc3wcP-F6Elo.js"></script> loaded unconditionally; no consent banner present
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 6 violations carry fines up to €10M or 2% of global annual turnover. ICO and national DPAs actively enforce cookie consent requirements.

findings.title: No gallery email allowlist — unauthenticated access to galleries
findings.description: The auth_view handler contains a TODO comment explicitly noting: 'Currently any syntactically valid email grants a JWT for any gallery, which is effectively unauthenticated access.' Any user can enter any email and receive a valid JWT to browse any gallery.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Add 'allowed_emails' or 'allowed_domains' field to each gallery. 2) Validate submitted email against allowlist before issuing JWT. 3) For public galleries, explicitly set 'is_public' flag. 4) Log access attempts and alert on suspicious patterns.
findings.evidence: TODO (CLIENT-03): Currently any syntactically valid email grants a JWT for any gallery, which is effectively unauthenticated access.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 6(1) requires a lawful basis. Without access controls, client data is exposed to unauthorized parties. Clients expect galleries to be private; unauthorized access constitutes a data breach.

findings.title: Privacy policy incomplete — missing key GDPR disclosures
findings.description: Privacy policy lacks GDPR-required disclosures: DPO contact, explicit retention periods, international transfer disclosure, legal basis per purpose, automated decision-making details, and data subject rights information.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Add 'Retention' section with specific periods for each data type. 2) Add 'Legal Basis' section. 3) Add 'Contact for data subject requests' email. 4) Add 'Data Protection Officer' section. 5) Disclose AWS data processing and transfers. 6) Add 'Data Subject Rights' section.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lacks DPO contact, retention detail, transfer disclosure, legal basis, and data subject rights information.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 13 & 14 violations; supervisory authorities can issue fines. Lack of transparency undermines user trust.

findings.title: Data subject access/export endpoints are admin-only, not user-accessible
findings.description: GDPR endpoints (/admin/gdpr/search, /admin/gdpr/erase) are super-admin protected. Photographers have no self-service mechanism to comply with data subject access requests. This creates operational friction and potential GDPR compliance issues.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Create user-accessible /account/data-export endpoint. 2) Implement 'My Data' dashboard. 3) Add 'Delete My Account' option. 4) Document SAR fulfillment procedures. 5) Set up privacy-requests@analogifystudio.com email.
findings.evidence: GDPR endpoints require X-Admin-Key authentication. No user-facing data export endpoints exist.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 15 & 20 give data subjects right to access data within 30 days. Photographers using Analogify cannot fulfill SARs without developer involvement.
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 15, 20 (Right of Access & Data Portability)

findings.title: Gallery access control missing privacy-by-design elements
findings.description: Galleries are created without explicit privacy settings. No default 'private' mode requiring photographers to opt-in to public access. No consent withdrawal mechanism for clients.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Add 'privacy_level' field (private/link-only/public). 2) Default galleries to 'private' mode. 3) Allow clients to revoke access. 4) Implement consent withdrawal.
findings.evidence: Gallery creation lacks privacy_level, allowed_recipients, or public/private flag.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 25 requires privacy by design. Photographers might unintentionally expose client data.
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 25 (Data Protection by Design and Default)

findings.title: No public Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with AWS
findings.description: Application uses AWS services without publicizing the Data Processing Agreement terms. Privacy policy does not link AWS DPA or explain processor role.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Link AWS Data Processing Addendum in privacy policy. 2) Clarify photographer as controller, Analogify/AWS as processor. 3) Disclose AWS region(s). 4) Explain transfer mechanism (SCCs, adequacy decision).
findings.evidence: Privacy policy mentions AWS generically but does not link DPA or explain processor status.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 28 non-compliance. Photographers may not realize they are liable for ensuring DPA is in place.

findings.title: Vague data retention policy; no documented retention schedule
findings.description: Privacy policy states 'about 30 days' for comments but no formal Data Retention Schedule is published. Different tables have different TTLs not communicated to users.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Create 'Data Retention Schedule' table in privacy policy. 2) List each data type, retention period, deletion method. 3) Add section 'When is my data deleted?' explaining TTL and procedures.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy: 'about 30 days' for comments; Terraform shows TTLs (access_log 1 year, jobs 90 days) not published to users.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) violation. Lack of transparent retention policies undermines compliance posture.

findings.title: Overly permissive CORS configuration
findings.description: auth_view handler defaults CORS_ORIGIN to '*' (wildcard). Allows any origin to make requests to API. Violates defense-in-depth principles.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Change default to specific origin (https://galleries.analogifystudio.com). 2) Set CORS_ORIGIN explicitly in Terraform. 3) Use whitelist, not wildcard. 4) Apply to all Lambda handlers.
findings.evidence: "Access-Control-Allow-Origin": os.environ.get("CORS_ORIGIN", "*")
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.5.1 requires access control. Wildcard CORS is a known attack vector.

findings.title: CSP allows 'unsafe-inline' for scripts
findings.description: CloudFront CSP includes 'unsafe-inline' for scripts, weakening XSS protection. Allows inline scripts, negating CSP defense.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Replace 'unsafe-inline' with nonces or hashes. 2) Generate unique nonce per response. 3) Test CSP in report-only mode first.
findings.evidence: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://analytics.codyjo.com
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.25 requires output encoding. 'unsafe-inline' significantly weakens XSS protection.

findings.title: No centralized logging of authentication/authorization events
findings.description: Lambda handlers log to CloudWatch but no centralized audit trail or SIEM integration. No monitoring/alerting for security events.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Set up CloudWatch Insights queries for auth failures. 2) Create alarms for suspicious patterns. 3) Integrate with Security Hub or SIEM. 4) Document incident response procedures.
findings.evidence: Logging to CloudWatch only; no centralized monitoring, alerting, or audit trail aggregation.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.16 requirement. Lack of centralized logging makes incident detection difficult.

findings.title: No public incident response or breach notification contact
findings.description: Privacy policy does not provide contact method for reporting security incidents. No security.txt file for researchers to report vulnerabilities.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Create /.well-known/security.txt per RFC 9110. 2) Add to privacy: 'Report vulnerabilities to security@analogifystudio.com'. 3) Document vulnerability disclosure policy. 4) Set up autoresponder.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lists general contact (cody@codyjo.com) but not specifically for security incidents.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.37 requirement. Lack of security contact makes responsible vulnerability disclosure harder.

findings.title: No published DPO contact information
findings.description: Privacy policy does not mention DPO status or contact. If DPO is appointed, contact must be published per GDPR.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Add: 'Analogify does not have a mandatory DPO' or 'DPO contact: dpo@analogifystudio.com'. 2) Make DPO contact accessible in footer. 3) Review if DPO appointment becomes required with scaling.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lists contact as 'cody@codyjo.com' without specifying DPO status.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 13(2)(b) & 14(2)(b) transparency requirement if DPO appointed.

findings.title: Missing Privacy Policy Documentation and Transparency
findings.description: The auth-service handles substantial personal data (email, name, password hashes, encryption keys) but there is no reference to a privacy policy document, no disclosure of data purposes, recipients, or retention periods. While the code mentions 'privacyPolicyVersion' in registration, there is no actual policy linked or enforced via the API.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Create and publish a comprehensive privacy policy covering: data controller/DPO contact, purposes of processing, categories of data, recipients, retention periods, data subject rights, GDPR legal basis. 2) Add a /api/auth/privacy-policy endpoint that returns the current policy version and URL. 3) Maintain policy version history in documentation with changelog. 4) Link privacy policy on client-side registration pages (handled by consuming apps). 5) Document GDPR legal basis (likely Article 6(1)(a) - consent, or Article 6(1)(b) - contract).
findings.evidence: Line 356 references 'privacyPolicyVersion: "2026-03"' but no privacy policy URL or endpoint exists. Users cannot access transparent information about how their data is processed.
findings.impact: Violation of GDPR Articles 13-14 (transparency requirements). Non-compliance can result in fines up to €10 million or 2% of annual global turnover, whichever is higher. Regulatory action by data protection authorities (e.g., DPA, ICO).

findings.title: No Data Portability / Data Export Endpoint
findings.description: Users have a right to receive their personal data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format (JSON, CSV, XML). The auth-service provides no endpoint to export user data, violating the right to data portability.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Add a new endpoint /api/auth/data-export (GET, requires auth token) that returns user profile, consent records, and activity metadata in JSON format. 2) Include email, name, userId, emailVerified, createdAt, consentedAt, passwordChangedAt, and any app-specific data linked via userId. 3) Return data within 30 days of request. 4) Implement audit logging of data export requests. 5) Consider rate limiting to prevent data scraping.
findings.evidence: Reviewed all API endpoints (lines 269-292). There is no /api/auth/export-data or similar endpoint. Users cannot exercise their right to data portability.
findings.impact: GDPR Article 20 violation. Users can file complaints with supervisory authorities. Fines up to €10 million or 2% turnover. Reputational damage and user trust erosion.

findings.title: No Documented Data Retention Policy
findings.description: GDPR requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary. There is no documented retention policy for user profiles, verification codes, reset codes, or other personal data. Data appears to be retained indefinitely unless explicitly deleted.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Define and document a data retention policy: user profiles (retained for account lifetime + 90 days after deletion for legal holds), verification codes (24 hours, already implemented), reset codes (15 minutes, already implemented), admin audit logs (2 years). 2) Implement automatic TTL deletion in DynamoDB for infrequently accessed accounts (e.g., accounts inactive for 3+ years) after user notification. 3) Add a retention_policy.md file documenting how long each data type is kept and why. 4) Provide a /api/auth/account/export endpoint for users to download their data before automatic deletion.
findings.evidence: User profiles are stored with no TTL. Verification codes have 24-hour TTL (line 370: expiresAt: now + 86400), reset codes have 15-minute TTL (line 508: expiresAt: now + 900), but user profile data has no automatic expiration. Manual deletion via /api/account DELETE endpoint is the only removal mechanism.
findings.impact: GDPR Article 5(1)(e) violation. Supervisory authorities may order deletion of non-essential retention. Fines up to €10 million or 2% turnover. Class action lawsuits from users.

findings.title: No DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment) Documentation
findings.description: The auth-service processes personal data related to authentication, encryption, and admin management. This system should have a DPIA to evaluate compliance risks and mitigations. No DPIA documentation is present in the codebase.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Conduct a DPIA covering: data flows, third-party recipients (AWS, Resend), security controls, risk assessments, mitigation measures. 2) Document high-risk areas: JWT secret storage in Secrets Manager, encryption key management, admin access to user data, email transmission via Resend. 3) Create a dpia.md file in the repository documenting the assessment, risks identified, and mitigations. 4) Review annually or when significant changes are made (new processors, new data types, new purposes).
findings.evidence: No DPIA documentation found in the repository. The system processes passwords, encryption keys, and user identifiers—all sensitive data that warrants risk assessment.
findings.impact: GDPR Article 35 requires a DPIA for processing likely to result in high risk. Absence of DPIA is a compliance violation. Supervisory authorities may require one and impose fines. Demonstrates lack of data protection governance.

findings.title: No Documented Data Processing Agreements (DPAs)
findings.description: The auth-service uses third-party processors (AWS DynamoDB, AWS Secrets Manager, Resend email service). GDPR requires Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) or Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) with all processors. No DPA documentation is present.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Verify that AWS has executed a Data Processing Addendum (DPA) as part of the AWS Business Associate Agreement (BAA) or Data Processing Agreement. 2) Obtain a DPA from Resend or verify SCCs are in place for email processing. 3) Create a dpa-compliance.md file documenting: which processors are used, what data they process, DPA/SCC dates, and review schedule. 4) Maintain an inventory of sub-processors (e.g., CDN, logging) and ensure consent for sub-processor changes is documented.
findings.evidence: Repository does not document DPAs with AWS or Resend. Terraform configuration shows use of these services, but no SCCs or DPA documentation is included.
findings.impact: GDPR Article 28 violation. Using processors without valid DPAs/SCCs is a serious compliance gap. Fines up to €10 million or 2% turnover. Supervisory authorities may demand evidence of DPAs. Personal data may be considered unlawfully transferred if SCCs are absent.

findings.title: Consent Collection Lacks Granularity and Transparency
findings.description: The registration endpoint requires a 'consent' boolean parameter but does not clarify what the user is consenting to or provide granular consent options (e.g., separate consent for email marketing, analytics, etc.). The consent mechanism is a binary checkbox without layered notices.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Expand consent collection to include granular options: 'I consent to account creation and authentication' (essential), 'I consent to marketing emails' (optional), 'I consent to analytics' (optional). 2) Add a /api/auth/consent endpoint to withdraw or modify consent at any time. 3) Store consent records with: timestamp, version of policy/terms, specific consents given (as a JSON object), IP address, and user agent. 4) Implement a preference center where users can manage their consent selections. 5) Ensure privacy policy is presented during registration with plain language explaining data use.
findings.evidence: Line 305: 'if (!consent) return res(400, { error: "Consent to privacy policy is required" }); '. The code does not specify what consent entails, and there is no mechanism for withdrawing consent granularly.
findings.impact: GDPR Articles 7, 12-14. Consent must be freely given, specific, and informed. Generic checkbox consent is insufficient under GDPR. Supervisory authorities may require more explicit consent mechanisms. Users may challenge consent validity.

findings.title: No Documented Breach Notification Process
findings.description: GDPR requires notification of data breaches to the supervisory authority within 72 hours and to affected individuals. There is no documented incident response plan, breach notification templates, or mechanisms for detecting and reporting breaches.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Create an incident-response.md file documenting: breach definitions, detection procedures, notification templates for supervisory authorities and users, escalation procedures. 2) Set up CloudWatch alarms for: failed authentication attempts (pattern: >10 in 1 minute), unauthorized DynamoDB queries, Secrets Manager access failures, Lambda errors. 3) Document a 72-hour breach notification timeline and designate responsible parties. 4) Implement a audit log for all data access events with timestamps, user IDs, and actions. 5) Schedule quarterly breach response drills.
findings.evidence: No incident response documentation, breach notification templates, or alerting configuration found. The CLAUDE.md mentions CloudWatch logs (/codebuild/auth-service) but no breach detection or alerting strategy.
findings.impact: GDPR Articles 33-34. Failure to notify within 72 hours can result in fines up to €10 million or 2% turnover. Supervisory authorities may investigate. Users may claim damages.

findings.title: Excessive Data Collection in Admin Operations
findings.description: The admin list-users endpoint returns full user profiles including sensitive fields. The endpoint scans up to 10 pages of the database without respecting data minimization principles. Admin users could potentially enumerate all user data.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Limit the number of scan pages to 3-5 instead of 10 to reduce database load and data exposure. 2) Consider indexing by createdAt to support pagination by date rather than full table scans. 3) Filter user list responses to return only: userId, email, name, emailVerified, disabled (omit sensitive fields). 4) Implement audit logging for admin list operations with query parameters. 5) Document why each field is returned to admins.
findings.evidence: Lines 108-154: The handleListUsers function scans PROFILE items across up to 10 database pages (MAX_SCAN_PAGES=10). While it returns limited fields to admins, the scan itself touches all profile data in the database.
findings.impact: GDPR Article 5(1)(c) requires data minimization. Excessive scanning increases breach risk and may violate the principle. Supervisory authorities may question why such broad scans are necessary.

findings.title: Missing Security Headers on API Responses
findings.description: API responses lack critical security headers (Content-Security-Policy, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, Cache-Control). These headers prevent common web vulnerabilities like XSS, clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and cache poisoning.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Add security headers to the res() function: 'Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains' (enforce HTTPS for 1 year). 2) Add 'X-Frame-Options: DENY' to prevent clickjacking. 3) Add 'X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff' to prevent MIME sniffing. 4) Add 'Content-Security-Policy: default-src \'self\'; script-src \'none\'; object-src \'none\''. 5) Add 'Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin'. 6) Add 'Permissions-Policy: geolocation=(), microphone=(), camera=()'. 7) For sensitive endpoints, add 'Cache-Control: private, no-store' to prevent caching of auth tokens.
findings.evidence: Lines 116-122: The corsHeaders function returns only CORS headers and Content-Type. No security headers are included in any response. Example missing headers: 'Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000', 'X-Frame-Options: DENY', 'Content-Security-Policy: default-src \'self\''.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.9 violation. Missing headers increase vulnerability to OWASP Top 10 attacks (XSS, clickjacking, MIME sniffing). Regulatory audits and penetration tests will flag this. Increases breach likelihood.

findings.title: Incomplete Audit Logging for Sensitive Operations
findings.description: While the code logs authentication and admin events to console (JSON format), there is no persistent, immutable audit trail. Logs are sent to CloudWatch but not configured for immutability or long-term retention. Some sensitive operations (e.g., encryption key changes) lack detailed audit context.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Configure CloudWatch Logs retention policy to at least 2 years for all /codebuild/auth-service logs. 2) Enable CloudWatch Logs encryption at rest. 3) Consider forwarding logs to a centralized system (e.g., AWS CloudTrail, Splunk, Datadog) for immutability and analysis. 4) Add audit logging context to password change operations (line 727): log 'ip', 'timestamp', 'action', 'affected_fields' (e.g., 'password_hash_changed'). 5) Log all admin actions with admin email, timestamp, target user, and action taken. 6) Implement log integrity checks (e.g., hash verification) to detect tampering.
findings.evidence: Lines 152, 172, 208, 257, 388, 417, 441, 525, 589, 644, 700, 737, 860, 887, 928: Audit logs are written to console.log(JSON.stringify(...)). There is no indication these logs are configured for immutability, long-term retention (e.g., 2 years), or forwarded to a centralized logging system.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.15/16 requires comprehensive audit trails. GDPR Article 5(2) requires accountability (demonstrable compliance). Without persistent audit logs, you cannot prove regulatory compliance or investigate incidents. Auditors will flag this gap.

findings.title: JWT Tokens Not Invalidated on Password Change
findings.description: When a user changes their password, existing JWT tokens remain valid until expiry (30 days). An attacker with a stolen token can continue accessing the account even after the password is changed. While passwordChangedAt invalidation is checked on token use, this is insufficient for true token invalidation.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Implement a token revocation list (revocation store in DynamoDB) where invalidated token IDs are tracked. 2) Add a 'jti' (JWT ID) claim to tokens that uniquely identifies each token. 3) When changing password (line 727), also: increment a 'tokenVersion' field on the user profile, check this version on token validation. 4) On logout (if implemented), delete the token from the revocation list or increment tokenVersion. 5) Alternatively, reduce JWT expiry from 30 days to 24 hours and require refresh tokens (which can be revoked immediately). 6) Document this strategy in security.md.
findings.evidence: Lines 173-176: Token validity is checked against passwordChangedAt, which is a mitigation but not true invalidation. A token issued before a password change will fail validation (token.iat < passwordChangedAt), but this relies on the server always checking this condition. Tokens are not explicitly revoked.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.2 requires secure session management and credential invalidation. OWASP Top 10 A07:2021 (Identification and Authentication Failures). If a password is compromised, changing it should immediately revoke all sessions. Current design increases breach impact window.

findings.title: No Explicit API Rate Limiting on All Endpoints
findings.description: While some endpoints have rate limiting (login: 5 attempts per 15 min, register: 3 per hour, password change: 5 per 15 min), other sensitive endpoints like /api/auth/password (PUT), /api/auth/email (PUT), and admin endpoints lack comprehensive rate limiting.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Add rate limiting to /api/auth/email (PUT): 3 attempts per hour per user. 2) Add rate limiting to /api/auth/name (PUT): 10 attempts per hour per user. 3) Add rate limiting to admin endpoints: /api/admin/users (GET): 100 per minute per admin, /api/admin/users/detail (GET): 1000 per minute per admin, other admin actions: 10 per minute per admin. 4) Use consistent rate limit keys (userId or admin email) across all endpoints. 5) Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header.
findings.evidence: Line 287: handleChangePassword has rate limiting (line 711). Lines 742-808: handleChangeEmail does NOT have rate limiting. Admin endpoints (lines 91-97) have no rate limiting outside of implicit DynamoDB limits.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.5.15 requires access controls. Lack of rate limiting increases risk of brute force attacks, account enumeration, and DoS. OWASP Top 10 A07:2021.

findings.title: Weak Password Policy - No Complexity Requirements
findings.description: The auth-service enforces a minimum password length of 10 characters but does not require password complexity (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters). This allows weak passwords like '1111111111' or 'aaaaaaaaaa'.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Update password validation regex to require: minimum 12 characters (or keep 10 with complexity), at least one uppercase letter, at least one lowercase letter, at least one digit, at least one special character (!@#$%^&*). 2) Check passwords against a breached password database (e.g., HaveIBeenPwned API) to prevent users from choosing commonly breached passwords. 3) Document password policy in privacy policy / terms of service. 4) Return helpful error messages: 'Password must contain uppercase, lowercase, digit, and special character' instead of generic 'password too weak'.
findings.evidence: Lines 307, 533, 708: Password validation checks only 'password.length < 10'. No regex or complexity validation is performed. A user could register with password '0000000000' and it would pass validation.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.2 recommends password complexity. NIST SP 800-63B recommends minimum 12 characters OR complexity. Weak passwords increase breach risk and compromise users' other accounts (password reuse).

findings.title: Encryption Keys Not Validated on Storage
findings.description: When users set or recover encryption keys (lines 838, 866), the API accepts and stores encrypted key material without validation. There is no verification that the key material is properly formatted, encrypted, or of expected length. This could allow storage of invalid or corrupted encryption data.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Add validation for encryptedMasterKey: minimum length 16 bytes, maximum length 4096 bytes, base64-encoded. 2) Add validation for masterKeyIv: exactly 16 bytes (128 bits for AES), base64-encoded. 3) Add validation for encryptionSalt: minimum 16 bytes, maximum 64 bytes, base64-encoded. 4) Add optional validations: recoveryKeyHash (if present, minimum 32 bytes), recoveryEncryptedMasterKey and recoveryKeyIv (same as primary key requirements). 5) Log warnings if key material exceeds expected sizes. 6) Consider adding a checksum or HMAC to detect corruption.
findings.evidence: Lines 838-858: handleSetEncryptionKeys accepts encryptedMasterKey, masterKeyIv, encryptionSalt without any validation. No length checks, no format validation, no encryption algorithm verification.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.24 requires proper cryptographic controls. Storing invalid key material could lead to permanent data loss or inability to decrypt user data. Increases support burden and user trust loss.

findings.title: No Documented Secret Rotation Policy for JWT Secret and API Keys
findings.description: The JWT secret and Resend API key are stored in AWS Secrets Manager with no documented rotation schedule. There is no procedure for rotating these secrets or preventing secret leakage impact.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1) Document a secret rotation policy in a security-operations.md file: JWT secret rotated every 90 days, Resend API key rotated annually or immediately if suspected compromise. 2) Implement secret versioning in Secrets Manager (already supported). 3) Update Lambda environment to fetch the latest secret version (currently always fetches latest, which is good). 4) Create a reminder/notification for quarterly secret rotation. 5) Test secret rotation in a staging environment before production rollout. 6) Document a compromise response plan: rotate JWT secret, force re-authentication of all active users (by invalidating tokens), rotate Resend API key.
findings.evidence: Lines 122-135 (terraform/lambda.tf): JWT secret is created and initially set but no rotation policy or versioning strategy is documented. No indication of how to rotate the secret or invalidate old tokens.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.32 requires change management and secret rotation. If a JWT secret is compromised, old tokens remain valid forever (30-day window). Increases breach impact. No documented rotation process indicates poor key management hygiene.

findings.title: API key stored in plaintext in configuration file
findings.description: The Back Office API key is stored as plaintext in api-config.yaml. While the file may be in .gitignore, it is still accessible on the filesystem to any user with file access. A compromised config file exposes the API key, allowing unauthorized access to all Back Office API endpoints including scan triggering and agent control.
findings.fix_suggestion: Move API key to environment variables. Update config.py to load from: os.getenv('BACKOFFICE_API_KEY', '')
findings.evidence: config file contains api_key field with plaintext string value
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.24 requires cryptographic protection of secrets. A compromised API key allows unauthorized API access and resource consumption.

findings.title: API key stored in plaintext in configuration file
findings.description: The Back Office API key is stored as plaintext in backoffice.example.yaml. While the file may be in .gitignore, it is still accessible on the filesystem to any user with file access. A compromised config file exposes the API key, allowing unauthorized access to all Back Office API endpoints including scan triggering and agent control.
findings.fix_suggestion: Move API key to environment variables. Update config.py to load from: os.getenv('BACKOFFICE_API_KEY', '')
findings.evidence: config file contains api_key field with plaintext string value
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.24 requires cryptographic protection of secrets. A compromised API key allows unauthorized API access and resource consumption.

findings.title: API key stored in plaintext in configuration file
findings.description: The Back Office API key is stored as plaintext in backoffice.yaml. While the file may be in .gitignore, it is still accessible on the filesystem to any user with file access. A compromised config file exposes the API key, allowing unauthorized access to all Back Office API endpoints including scan triggering and agent control.
findings.fix_suggestion: Move API key to environment variables. Update config.py to load from: os.getenv('BACKOFFICE_API_KEY', '')
findings.evidence: config file contains api_key field with plaintext string value
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.24 requires cryptographic protection of secrets. A compromised API key allows unauthorized API access and resource consumption.

findings.title: API server does not enforce TLS/HTTPS for sensitive data transmission
findings.description: The Back Office API server can bind to 0.0.0.0 and has no mandatory TLS enforcement. API authentication uses HMAC with X-API-Key header transmitted in plaintext HTTP, allowing network eavesdropping and credential capture.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement TLS using Python's ssl module. Generate self-signed certificates or use reverse proxy (nginx). Restrict bind address to 127.0.0.1 by default.
findings.evidence: No ssl.SSLContext usage found; api_server binds to 0.0.0.0 by default
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.24 requires encryption in transit for sensitive data. Credentials over HTTP expose auth keys to MITM attacks.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.24 (Encryption in Transit), A.5.17 (Access Control)

findings.title: No API key rotation mechanism or expiry policy
findings.description: API keys appear to be long-lived static credentials with no automatic expiry or rotation. Keys lack metadata (created_at, expires_at) and there is no mechanism to deprecate or invalidate old keys.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement key versioning with metadata. Add key expiry (90 days). Implement rotation command and document policy.
findings.evidence: api_key is a plain string with no ttl, expiry_date, or rotation_date field
findings.impact: Long-lived static credentials increase breach window. If compromised, there is no automatic invalidation mechanism.

findings.title: File permissions not explicitly set on created findings files
findings.description: Results files are created without explicit permission settings. Default umask might create world-readable files containing audit findings and system information.
findings.fix_suggestion: After creating results files, set restrictive permissions: os.chmod(path, 0o600) or 0o640 for group read
findings.evidence: json.dump() without explicit mode/chmod after file creation
findings.impact: World-readable findings files could expose audit data to unauthorized users on shared systems.

findings.title: No documentation of Data Processing Agreements with third-party processors
findings.description: Back-Office uses Anthropic (Claude API) and AWS services as data processors. There is no documented evidence of Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) or clauses restricting how these processors handle findings data.
findings.fix_suggestion: Obtain/verify Data Processing Addendum from Anthropic and AWS. Document processor details and retention policies in README or COMPLIANCE.md
findings.evidence: No DPA or processor contract documentation found
findings.impact: Without DPAs, there is no contractual control over how third parties use audit findings. May violate ISO 27001 and compliance policies.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.9 (Systems access control), Industry practice

findings.title: No documented backup and disaster recovery procedures for audit data
findings.description: Back-Office stores persistent audit findings in backlog.json and dashboard data in dashboard/ directory. There is no documented backup strategy, disaster recovery procedures, or RTO/RPO targets for these critical audit records.
findings.fix_suggestion: Document backup strategy: daily backups of backlog.json to S3 with versioning enabled. Define RTO (1 day) and RPO (24 hours).
findings.evidence: No backup procedures documented; no RTO/RPO targets specified
findings.impact: Loss of audit data could impair ability to track remediation progress and audit history. ISO 27001 A.8.13 requires backup controls.

findings.title: No rate limiting on API endpoints
findings.description: The API endpoints (/api/run-scan, /api/run-all, etc.) have no rate limiting. An attacker with an API key could trigger unlimited scan jobs, consuming resources and potentially causing DoS.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement per-key rate limiting: e.g., 10 scans per minute per API key. Use sliding window counter or token bucket algorithm.
findings.evidence: No rate limiting or request throttling logic in API handlers
findings.impact: Lack of rate limiting enables DoS attacks and resource exhaustion.

findings.title: No documented data retention policy for audit findings and backlog
findings.description: Back-Office stores audit findings indefinitely in backlog.json with no documented retention period or purging strategy. Historical findings are never deleted, creating unbounded storage growth and retention of potentially outdated information.
findings.fix_suggestion: Define retention policy: keep last 12 months of findings, archive older findings monthly. Implement pruning in update_score_history().
findings.evidence: No code or documentation specifying TTL or deletion policy for old findings
findings.impact: Indefinite data retention may violate data minimization principles. Outdated findings may be mistakenly used in future decisions.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.13 (Backup), Industry Standard Data Governance

gdpr.notes: Back Office is an internal administrative tool and does not directly process personal data from end users. GDPR does not apply to the Back Office system itself, but Back Office audits services that are GDPR-regulated.

age_verification.notes: Back Office is an internal audit tool, not a consumer-facing service. Age verification laws do not apply.

findings.title: No age verification gate despite stated 16+ age requirement
findings.description: The privacy policy states 'account registration is intended only for people who are at least 16 years old,' but the registration flow contains no age verification, date-of-birth collection, or age gate. US state laws (Utah S.B. 152, Texas HB 1181, Virginia SB 1515, Arkansas SB 396) and California AADC (AB 2273) require age verification for online platforms accessible to minors.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement age verification gate: (1) Add date-of-birth field to registration form. (2) Validate server-side to confirm user is 16+ (preferably 18+). (3) Consider third-party age verification (Yoti, Jumio, Veriff). (4) Store verification status in DynamoDB. (5) Update privacy policy with verification mechanism. (6) Implement jurisdiction-specific age rules for US users.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy line 81 states age requirement, but lambda/api/index.mjs#359-440 (handleRegister) accepts any email/password/name without age verification. No date-of-birth field or third-party age verification integration.
findings.impact: Exposure to enforcement by state attorneys general. Potential fines $7,500+ per violation. Reputational damage if platform accessed by minors in violation of stated policy.
findings.regulation: Utah S.B. 152, Texas HB 1181, Virginia SB 1515, Arkansas SB 396, California AB 2273

findings.title: No explicit data processing consent collection
findings.description: No evidence of explicit consent collection for data processing. Privacy policy exists but lacks: (1) interactive consent banner/modal at registration; (2) granular consent choices (essential vs. analytics vs. marketing); (3) consent confirmation records with timestamp and version; (4) consent withdrawal mechanism. GDPR Art. 6 requires lawful basis for processing; Art. 7 mandates consent be freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement consent collection: (1) Add consent banner/modal to registration with clear descriptions. (2) Require explicit checkboxes (not pre-checked) for each optional consent. (3) Store consent records in DynamoDB with userId, date, version, scopes. (4) Add preference center for consent management. (5) Log consent changes for audit.
findings.evidence: Registration form (register/page.tsx#51-82) collects name, email, password without consent checkboxes or privacy policy acknowledgment. No consent records in DynamoDB schema.
findings.impact: GDPR violations: fines up to €20M or 4% of annual global revenue. Data subject complaints to supervisory authorities triggering investigation.

findings.title: No Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with third parties
findings.description: Personal data transferred to multiple third parties without documented DPAs: (1) Anthropic (Claude API for tutoring) - lambda/tutor/index.mjs sends user questions to Claude. (2) Resend (email) - lambda/api/index.mjs#204-225 sends user emails to Resend API. (3) Plausible (analytics) - src/app/layout.tsx includes Plausible script. (4) AWS (S3, DynamoDB) - US-based infrastructure. Privacy policy mentions transfers vaguely but lacks: (a) confirmation of SCCs/BCRs; (b) transparency about transfer safeguards; (c) complete recipient list.
findings.fix_suggestion: Establish DPAs and document safeguards: (1) Obtain signed Data Processing Agreements from Anthropic, Resend, Plausible, AWS. (2) Confirm SCCs in place with each vendor. (3) Update privacy policy: explicitly list recipients (Anthropic, Resend, Plausible, AWS) with locations and roles. (4) Add section 'International Data Transfers' explaining SCCs/safeguards. (5) For EU users, display transfer notice or offer EU-only option. (6) Document transfer mechanism in DPIA.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy (lines 56-59) is generic: 'CertStudy is hosted primarily on Amazon Web Services in the United States. AI tutor responses use Anthropic...' Lacks specificity about SCCs, DPAs, or complete recipient list. Plausible analytics not mentioned.
findings.impact: GDPR violations for international transfers: fines up to €20M or 4% of global revenue. Supervisory authority investigation and data subject complaints.

findings.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) documented
findings.description: CertStudy processes personal data through high-risk activities requiring DPIA per GDPR Art. 35: (1) Large-scale processing of special categories (educational records, learning patterns). (2) Systematic monitoring (quiz results, flashcard performance tracking). (3) International transfer to US. (4) AI/automated decision-making (Claude API tutoring). (5) Long-term educational record storage. No DPIA documentation found.
findings.fix_suggestion: Commission or perform DPIA: (1) Document processing: purpose, data types, recipients, retention, technical/organizational measures. (2) Assess risks: unauthorized access, data breaches, profiling abuse, AI impact. (3) Identify mitigations: encryption, access controls, audit logs, data minimization. (4) Document findings in DPIA report. (5) Share with DPO if applicable. (6) Update annually.
findings.evidence: No DPIA documentation in repo. Privacy policy does not reference DPIA. Terraform config contains no DPIA references.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 35 violation: fines up to €20M or 4%. Supervisory authority investigation. DPIA supports compliance defense if breaches occur.

findings.title: Limited right to restrict processing and right to object mechanisms
findings.description: While account deletion and data export are implemented, mechanisms for right to restrict processing (Art. 18) and right to object (Art. 21) are missing. Privacy policy mentions rights generically but lacks specific implementation. Users cannot: (1) restrict processing without deletion; (2) opt out of analytics/profiling; (3) object to marketing or automated decision-making.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add data subject rights endpoints: (1) PATCH /api/account/processing to restrict processing. (2) PATCH /api/account/analytics-consent to opt out of analytics. (3) Add settings page for data rights management. (4) Implement correction workflow for inaccurate data. (5) Document prominently in privacy policy with direct links.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy (lines 75-77) mentions rights but only DELETE /api/account and GET /api/export are implemented. No endpoints for restricting processing or objecting to non-essential processing.
findings.impact: GDPR Arts. 18, 21 non-compliance: fines and enforcement orders to implement rights mechanisms.

findings.title: Excessive data collection in tutor/AI interactions
findings.description: Tutor Lambda (lambda/tutor/index.mjs#315-410) stores full conversation history without clear data minimization. Each session records: full user messages, full AI responses, learning context. While conversation history is necessary for feature functionality, data is not minimized and retention periods are unclear.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement data minimization: (1) Store only essential metadata (userId, sessionId, trackId, timestamp, duration). (2) Do NOT store full messages by default; optionally store with 7-day TTL. (3) Allow users to opt-in to history retention. (4) Implement 90-day retention policy with auto-deletion. (5) Provide UI to view/delete sessions. (6) Update privacy policy with tutor data retention policy.
findings.evidence: Tutor Lambda (line 359-410) stores full messages and responses in DynamoDB. No redaction, summarization, or compression. No documented retention period beyond generic TTL.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) data minimization violation: regulatory scrutiny, fines up to €20M or 4%.

findings.title: CSP allows unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval for scripts
findings.description: CloudFront CSP (terraform/cloudfront.tf#230-231) permits: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' https://plausible.io. 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' significantly weaken XSS protection, allowing attackers to inject malicious scripts more easily.
findings.fix_suggestion: Strengthen CSP: (1) Remove 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval'. (2) Use Next.js CSP with script hashes/nonces for inline scripts. (3) Example: script-src 'self' 'nonce-{random}' https://plausible.io. (4) Test updated CSP for violations. (5) Update terraform/cloudfront.tf with stricter policy.
findings.evidence: CSP header line 231: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' https://plausible.io.
findings.impact: Weak CSP increases XSS attack risk: credential theft, session hijacking, malware injection. ISO 27001 A.8.25 and GDPR Art. 32 require security measures.

findings.title: Limited logging of security-relevant events
findings.description: API Lambda includes minimal logging (logAuthEvent function). Missing: (1) failed authentication attempts; (2) authorization failures; (3) modification events; (4) data export/deletion details; (5) tutor session events; (6) error tracking; (7) IP/user-agent for forensics. ISO 27001 requires comprehensive audit logging with: event type, timestamp, actor, action, object, outcome, details.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement comprehensive audit logging: (1) Create auditLog() function capturing: timestamp, event type, userId, sourceIp, userAgent, action, object, outcome, details. (2) Log authentication (success/failure), authorization, data ops, errors, rate limits. (3) Store logs in CloudWatch with 30+ day retention. (4) Implement CloudWatch rules to alert on suspicious patterns. (5) Provide audit log download via API (restricted to admin).
findings.evidence: Logging function (line 235-237) is minimal: only console.log. Auth events logged but not failed attempts, authorization failures, or modifications.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 non-compliance. Inadequate logs impair breach investigation (required by GDPR Art. 33).

findings.title: Sensitive data stored unencrypted in localStorage
findings.description: Client-side code stores sensitive data in localStorage without encryption: (1) 'cert_token' (JWT cleartext); (2) 'cert_user' (profile cleartext); (3) 'cert_preferences' (cleartext); (4) 'cert_enc_cache' (encryption keys). If device is compromised, attackers can extract tokens and PII. GDPR Art. 25 requires privacy by design and high-privacy defaults.
findings.fix_suggestion: Encrypt sensitive localStorage data: (1) Derive encryption key from password (PBKDF2). (2) Use AES-256-GCM to encrypt 'cert_token', 'cert_user', 'cert_enc_cache'. (3) Prompt for password unlock on page load. (4) Use sessionStorage (expires on tab close) for tokens instead of localStorage. (5) Implement automatic logout on inactivity (30 minutes).
findings.evidence: Storage constants (lines 28-40) store data in localStorage without encryption. Token retrieval (auth-context.tsx line 25): localStorage.getItem(TOKEN_KEY) — no decryption.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 25 non-compliance. Device compromise → credential theft → breach. Breach notification and fines may result.
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 25 (Privacy by Design and Default)

findings.title: Privacy policy incomplete: missing AI profiling, data accuracy, analytics disclosure
findings.description: Privacy policy lacks: (1) automated decision-making/profiling disclosure; (2) data accuracy mechanisms; (3) exact retention periods (vague 'about 30 days'); (4) right to lodge complaint with supervisory authority; (5) complete recipient list (Plausible, Resend omitted); (6) analytics disclosure.
findings.fix_suggestion: Expand privacy policy: (1) Add 'Automated Processing': disclose Claude AI profiling for tutoring recommendations. (2) Add 'Data Accuracy' section: users can flag inaccurate data; correction within 7 days. (3) Specify exact retention: 'Operational logs: 30 days. Security logs: 90 days. Tutor sessions: 180 days.' (4) Add complaint right: 'Lodge complaint with supervisory authority [link].' (5) Complete recipient list: 'AWS, Anthropic, Resend, Plausible.' (6) Add analytics disclosure: 'Plausible (EU-based, no cookies, GDPR-compliant).' (7) Review annually.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy (lines 62-64) vaguely mentions 'AI boundaries' but not profiling. No mention of Plausible analytics. No supervisory authority complaint right. Retention periods vague ('about 30 days').
findings.impact: GDPR Arts. 13–14 non-compliance: incomplete transparency. Supervisory authority investigation, fines, enforcement orders.

findings.title: No multi-factor authentication (MFA) support
findings.description: Authentication uses password-only (single-factor). No MFA/2FA, TOTP, SMS OTP, recovery codes, or backup methods. While passwords use strong PBKDF2-SHA512, single-factor is insufficient for sensitive educational/PII data. ISO 27001 recommends MFA for sensitive systems.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement optional/mandatory MFA: (1) Add TOTP support (speakeasy library). (2) On login, prompt for TOTP if MFA enabled. (3) In settings: enable/disable MFA, generate recovery codes. (4) Add endpoints: POST /api/auth/mfa/setup, POST /api/auth/mfa/verify, POST /api/auth/mfa/disable. (5) Store TOTP secret (encrypted), MFA flag, recovery codes in DynamoDB. (6) Update UI: settings page MFA management.
findings.evidence: Login handler (line 442-475) accepts only email/password. No MFA. Registration does not set up MFA. No MFA fields in DynamoDB or API endpoints.
findings.impact: Increased account compromise risk via credential stuffing, phishing, brute force. Breach risk. GDPR breach notification may result.

findings.title: No age-appropriate design features for minor users
findings.description: Privacy policy claims 'not directed to children under 13' and '16+ registration', yet design lacks age-appropriate protections: (1) no content filters/parental controls; (2) no default high-privacy for minors; (3) no profiling/data-sharing restrictions; (4) no dark pattern protections; (5) no age-appropriate privacy info. California AB 2273 (AADC), UK OSA 2023, Ofcom Children's Code require age-appropriate design.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement age-appropriate design (requires architectural changes): (1) Age verification (see COMP-001). (2) For minors: high-privacy defaults (opt-in analytics), parental controls, study limits, no dark patterns. (3) For adults (18+): current design. (4) Restrict: profiling, third-party sharing for minors. (5) Parental controls: optional setup if parent creates child account. (6) No infinite scroll, autoplay, addictive design for minors.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy claims age requirements (line 80-82) but no code prevents child accounts, no content filters, no minor-specific privacy settings.
findings.impact: GDPR + California AB 2273 + UK Online Safety Act enforcement. Regulators can: order design changes, impose fines, restrict to adults-only.
findings.regulation: California AB 2273, UK Online Safety Act 2023, Ofcom Children's Code

findings.title: Verbose error messages may leak sensitive information
findings.description: API error messages leak information enabling account enumeration, validation attacks: (1) 'Email not found vs. password incorrect' distinction; (2) 'Password must be at least 10 characters' reveals policy; (3) 'Invalid email format' validates email externally. Attackers can enumerate accounts, reverse-engineer API constraints.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use generic error messages: (1) Auth failures: 'Invalid email or password' (don't distinguish user existence). (2) Validation errors: 'Invalid request' (don't reveal specific failures to unauthenticated users). (3) Log detailed errors server-side. (4) Detailed errors only to authenticated users. (5) Example: delete account should return 'Authentication failed' not 'Password is incorrect'.
findings.evidence: Error messages: line 362 'Password must be at least 10 characters'; line 387 generic response; line 815 'Password is incorrect'; line 367 'Invalid email format'.
findings.impact: Information disclosure increases account compromise risk, potential breach. ISO 27001 A.8.32 requires secure error handling.

findings.title: Plausible analytics not explicitly disclosed or consented to
findings.description: Plausible Analytics included in CSP and layout but not disclosed in privacy policy. While Plausible is GDPR-compliant (no cookies), GDPR Art. 7 requires clear consent, Art. 21 allows opt-out. Privacy policy should explicitly disclose analytics.
findings.fix_suggestion: Disclose analytics: (1) Add to privacy policy: 'We use Plausible Analytics (GDPR-compliant, no cookies) to understand site usage.' (2) Add opt-out: 'Opt out of analytics [link].' (3) Alternatively, implement consent banner with 'Allow analytics?' option. (4) Update privacy policy with opt-out method.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy (line 36-46) does not mention Plausible. Layout.tsx includes Plausible script but no consent mechanism.
findings.impact: GDPR non-compliance: processing without disclosed consent. Supervisory authority could find tracking non-compliant.

findings.title: No data subject rights mechanisms (access, deletion, portability)
findings.description: The codyjo.com website collects personal data through a contact form (name, email, message) and server logs (IP, user agent), but provides no mechanisms for users to exercise their fundamental GDPR rights: access to personal data (Art. 15), deletion of personal data (Art. 17), or data portability (Art. 20). These are mandatory rights in GDPR compliance. The privacy policy acknowledges these rights exist but provides no process to fulfill them.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Create a 'Download My Data' feature that exports all collected personal data (contact form submissions) as JSON/CSV. 2. Create an 'Account Deletion Request' mechanism (email-based workflow to process erasure). 3. Update privacy policy with clear contact information and process for exercising rights. 4. Implement backend data export/deletion endpoint accessible via authenticated API or email-based workflow. 5. Document data retention periods and implement automatic deletion after retention window.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy line 116-121 states 'You can request access to, correction of, or deletion of personal data held through this site' but no mechanism (email, form, portal) is provided. Contact form only collects data, does not provide data export or deletion.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 15-20 violations can result in regulatory enforcement action by data protection authorities, fines up to €10M or 2% of global turnover (whichever is higher), and individual user complaints to supervisory authorities. This is a fundamental compliance gap that creates immediate exposure.

findings.title: No data retention policy documented or implemented
findings.description: The privacy policy does not specify how long personal data collected through the contact form is retained. GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires storage limitation: personal data must be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary for the purposes for which the data are processed. There is no visible TTL, archive, or deletion mechanism for contact form data.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Define data retention policy: e.g., 'Contact form submissions are retained for 12 months for correspondence purposes, then automatically deleted.' 2. Implement TTL/expiry on contact data in backend storage (DynamoDB, S3, etc.). 3. Add explicit retention period to privacy policy (Section 2 or new section). 4. Document and test automatic deletion process (Lambda scheduled event, S3 lifecycle policy, etc.).
findings.evidence: Privacy policy does not mention data retention periods. Contact form sends data to AWS Lambda API (https://ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/contact) with no visible retention or deletion configuration.
findings.impact: Indefinite storage of personal data violates GDPR Art. 5 principles. Can trigger regulatory findings, enforcement requests, and demonstrates organizational neglect of data minimization obligations. Not technically a fine-level violation by itself, but evidence of systemic non-compliance.

findings.title: Consent mechanism lacks granularity and clear lawful basis
findings.description: The contact form collects a privacy consent checkbox, but the consent is bundled: users must agree to 'processing of data as described in the Privacy Policy' to submit the form. The privacy policy does not distinguish between different processing purposes (contact response vs. analytics vs. server logs) or provide granular consent options. Users cannot selectively consent to contact communication while opting out of analytics, for example.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Offer granular consent checkboxes separate from form submission: 'I agree to be contacted about my inquiry' (required to submit) vs. 'I consent to analytics' (optional). 2. Allow form submission without analytics consent. 3. Implement cookie consent banner or preference center to manage Plausible analytics separately from contact form consent. 4. Update privacy policy to clearly identify lawful basis for each processing purpose (consent for contact, legitimate interest for security logs, etc.).
findings.evidence: Consent checkbox (line 306-308) states: 'I agree to the processing of my data as described in the Privacy Policy.' No granular choices (analytics, marketing, contact response) are offered. Privacy policy (src/pages/privacy.astro) mentions Plausible analytics separately but does not offer opt-in/opt-out mechanism.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 6 requires consent to be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. Bundled consent (pre-ticked or required to proceed) is not freely given. Supervisory authorities in the EU (e.g., EDPB) have consistently found bundled consent inadequate. Can trigger enforcement action and fines up to €20M or 4% of global turnover.

findings.title: CSP allows unsafe-inline scripts, bypassing content security policy protections
findings.description: The Content Security Policy (CSP) header in Base.astro (line 66) sets `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'`, which allows inline JavaScript to execute without restriction. This defeats the primary purpose of CSP: preventing XSS attacks via script injection. Additionally, `style-src 'unsafe-inline'` allows inline stylesheets without validation.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Remove 'unsafe-inline' from script-src. Move inline scripts to external files or use nonces. 2. Remove 'unsafe-inline' from style-src. Extract inline styles to CSS files. 3. Use nonce or hash-based CSP for legitimate inline scripts (e.g., analytics initialization). 4. Set script-src to 'self' plus explicitly trusted CDNs (analytics.codyjo.com, etc.). 5. Test CSP enforcement: enable CSP in report-only mode first, then enforce.
findings.evidence: CSP header: `default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://analytics.codyjo.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; ...` The unsafe-inline directive allows any inline script/style to execute, including attacker-controlled input if XSS vulnerability exists.
findings.impact: While not a direct GDPR violation, unsafe CSP significantly increases the likelihood and impact of XSS vulnerabilities, which can lead to data theft, session hijacking, and exposure of sensitive user data (contact form submissions, session tokens). Makes the site vulnerable to defacement and malware injection.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.25 - Input Validation & Output Encoding

findings.title: Missing Cache-Control header on sensitive pages
findings.description: HTML pages and the contact form lack explicit Cache-Control headers to prevent caching of sensitive content. While CloudFront cache settings handle some caching, HTML pages served via S3 do not include Cache-Control: no-store or private headers to prevent browser caching of sensitive user data (contact form confirmation, etc.).
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Set Cache-Control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate on all HTML responses (handled via CloudFront behavior). 2. Verify S3 Object Metadata includes cache-control headers. 3. For authenticated/sensitive pages, use Cache-Control: private, no-store, max-age=0. 4. Add Astro middleware or CloudFront response headers policy to enforce caching rules.
findings.evidence: Base.astro layout does not set Cache-Control headers on responses. buildspec-cd.yml (lines 24-28) sets cache-control for HTML files: 'public, max-age=0, must-revalidate', which is correct but relies on CloudFront configuration. Direct S3 access bypasses this.
findings.impact: Without Cache-Control headers, sensitive information (contact form submissions, session data if added in future) can be cached by browsers and proxies, exposing user data to subsequent users on shared devices. Potential confidentiality violation under ISO 27001 and GDPR Art. 32 (security of processing).

findings.title: Privacy policy missing some required GDPR disclosures
findings.description: The privacy policy is comprehensive but lacks a few mandatory GDPR disclosures: (1) whether provision of personal data is a statutory or contractual requirement, (2) whether the service involves automated decision-making or profiling with legal/significant effects, (3) DPO contact information (if applicable), and (4) right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Add statement: 'You may voluntarily provide contact information to send an inquiry. Providing this information is optional; however, without it we cannot respond to your message.' 2. Add: 'We do not use automated decision-making or profiling.' (if true) or describe any such processing. 3. Add DPO contact info if applicable, or state 'No Data Protection Officer is designated for this site.' 4. Add: 'You have the right to lodge a complaint with your national data protection authority' and link to relevant authority (e.g., EDPB or UK ICO).
findings.evidence: Privacy policy sections cover lawful basis, third parties, and rights, but do not explicitly state: whether providing data is optional or required (the form implies it's required for contact), whether any automated decisions affect the user, who the Data Protection Officer is (if one exists), or which supervisory authority can be contacted (e.g., 'you can contact the [relevant EU] data protection authority').
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 13 requires transparency. Missing disclosures are a compliance gap but less severe than missing rights mechanisms. Can trigger supervisory authority requests for updated privacy information. Minor fine risk if enforcement occurs.

findings.title: No visible logging or audit trail configuration for contact form data
findings.description: The contact form sends data to an AWS Lambda API endpoint, but there is no visible logging configuration (CloudWatch logs, audit trails, or event logging) for contact form submissions. Without audit logging, it is impossible to detect unauthorized access, deletions, or modifications to personal data in compliance with ISO 27001 A.8.15 and GDPR Art. 32.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Configure CloudWatch logging for the Lambda function handling contact form submissions. 2. Log successful submissions with timestamp, source IP (for audit purposes), and field names (not sensitive values). 3. Log failed submissions and rate limit triggers. 4. Ensure logs are immutable and retained per data retention policy (minimum 1 year). 5. Implement log analysis/alerting for suspicious patterns (e.g., 10+ requests from same IP in 1 minute).
findings.evidence: Contact form submits to https://ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/contact (line 343) with no visible logging, error tracking, or audit trail. The form client-side code does not reference any logging; backend Lambda is not visible in codebase.
findings.impact: Without audit logs, you cannot prove compliance with data handling obligations, investigate breaches, or respond to regulatory requests. ISO 27001 A.8.15 and GDPR Art. 32 require security measures including logging and monitoring. Can result in enforcement findings and inability to demonstrate compliance.

findings.title: No visible Data Processing Agreement with Plausible Analytics
findings.description: The site uses Plausible Analytics for privacy-focused website analytics. While Plausible is GDPR-compliant, GDPR Art. 28 requires a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) between the controller (Cody Jo Method/codyjo.com) and any processor (Plausible). No DPA link or evidence is visible on the site.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Verify that you have signed Plausible's Data Processing Agreement (available on their website or via their legal terms). 2. Add statement to privacy policy: 'We have executed a Data Processing Agreement with Plausible Analytics in compliance with GDPR Article 28.' 3. Keep signed DPA in your records and make it available to data protection authorities upon request. 4. Similarly, verify DPAs exist for any other data processors (AWS, CDN providers, etc.).
findings.evidence: Analytics script (line 124): `<script is:inline async src="https://analytics.codyjo.com/js/pa-KlbqzsCUHElDKXDO42D4D.js"></script>` Privacy policy references Plausible (line 16-18) with link to data policy, but does not reference a DPA or binding contract.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 28 requires a written contract (DPA) between controller and processor. Without visible evidence of a DPA, a data protection authority could question whether the relationship is properly governed. Plausible's standard terms typically include a DPA, but you should verify you have signed it and can provide evidence.

findings.title: Contact form API lacks authentication and rate limiting is client-side only
findings.description: The contact form endpoint (https://ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/contact) accepts POST requests with no visible authentication (API key, OAuth, mutual TLS). Rate limiting is implemented client-side (429 error handling), but a determined attacker can bypass client-side checks by directly calling the API.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Implement server-side rate limiting using AWS WAF or API Gateway throttling (e.g., 10 requests per minute per IP). 2. Add CORS restrictions to API Gateway to limit requests to www.codyjo.com domain only. 3. Implement request signing or API key authentication if feasible (though not essential for public forms). 4. Add CAPTCHA or honeypot validation (honeypot already implemented, line 301-303, which is good). 5. Log all failed/rate-limited requests to CloudWatch.
findings.evidence: Contact form submits to unauthenticated public API endpoint (line 343). Rate limiting is handled client-side (lines 402-403: 'if (res.status === 429)'). No API key, authorization header, or authentication mechanism visible in request.
findings.impact: Without server-side rate limiting and authentication, the endpoint is vulnerable to spam, DoS attacks, and abuse (e.g., flooding the contact inbox or underlying database). While not a direct GDPR violation, it demonstrates lack of adequate security controls (ISO 27001 A.8.2) and increases risk of system compromise or data exposure.

findings.title: Contact form data transmitted over HTTPS but encryption at rest not documented
findings.description: Contact form data is transmitted to the API via HTTPS (TLS), which is correct. However, there is no visible documentation of how the contact form data is stored at rest in the backend (DynamoDB, S3, RDS, etc.) or whether it is encrypted.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Verify backend storage (Lambda -> DynamoDB/S3) uses encryption at rest: enable DynamoDB encryption (default is AES-256 via AWS KMS), enable S3 default encryption. 2. Document encryption status in security documentation or compliance records. 3. If storing contact data longer-term, consider field-level encryption for sensitive data (email, message content). 4. Add statement to privacy policy: 'Contact form submissions are encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest using AWS-managed encryption.'
findings.evidence: Contact form submits via HTTPS (line 392: `fetch(CONTACT_API, ...)`), which is secure in transit. However, backend storage method and encryption are not documented in the codebase (likely handled by AWS Lambda, but not visible).
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 32 and ISO 27001 A.8.24 require encryption of personal data at rest. Without documented encryption, you cannot demonstrate compliance with data protection obligations. If a breach occurs and data is exposed unencrypted, it is evidence of inadequate security measures.

findings.title: Permissions-Policy header not explicitly set
findings.description: The CSP header is comprehensive, but the Permissions-Policy (formerly Feature-Policy) header is not set. This allows all browser features (geolocation, camera, microphone) to be accessed by scripts on the page. While codyjo.com does not use these features, explicitly disabling them is a security best practice.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Add Permissions-Policy header: `Permissions-Policy: geolocation=(), camera=(), microphone=(), payment=()` to explicitly disable unnecessary browser features. 2. Add to CloudFront response headers policy or Base.astro meta tag.
findings.evidence: CSP header present but no Permissions-Policy header visible. Not a critical issue since the site does not intentionally use browser features, but explicit policy strengthens security posture.
findings.impact: No direct regulatory impact. However, if the site adds JavaScript libraries or third-party scripts in the future, an unset Permissions-Policy could allow malicious code to access sensitive features. Minimal exposure currently.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.9 - Security Headers / Best Practice

findings.title: Privacy policy could clarify third-party data sharing and international transfers
findings.description: Privacy policy mentions AWS hosting in the United States but does not provide Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), Adequacy Decisions, or detailed information about international transfers for EU users. GDPR Art. 44-49 require transparency and safeguards for cross-border transfers.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Add section to privacy policy: 'International Data Transfers: Personal data may be transferred to the United States for hosting and analytics. We rely on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) and AWS commitment to Standard Contractual Clauses for data protection.' 2. For Plausible: verify their SCCs and reference them in privacy policy. 3. Provide users the option to contact if they have concerns about transfers.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy line 43 states 'Infrastructure is primarily hosted in the United States' but does not explain the legal mechanism (SCCs, Adequacy Decision under Schrems II) or user rights regarding transfer. Plausible data policy link is provided (line 18) but privacy policy does not explicitly state that Plausible transfers data internationally.
findings.impact: GDPR requires transparency about international transfers and appropriate safeguards. Post-Schrems II, transfers to the US require SCCs plus supplementary measures. Lack of transparency can trigger supervisory authority findings but is typically lower-priority than missing rights mechanisms.

findings.title: Error messages may leak system information to users
findings.description: Contact form error messages (index.astro lines 400-407) include HTTP status codes (422, 429, 500) in user-facing messages. While these are helpful for debugging, detailed error information can leak system information to attackers. Example: 'Server error (500)' reveals that an application error occurred; 'HTTP 429' reveals rate limiting is in place.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. For user-facing errors: show generic message 'Something went wrong. Please try again later.' without status codes. 2. Log detailed error information (status code, stack trace) server-side for debugging. 3. Keep status codes in browser console (for developers) but not in user messages. Example: `if (res.ok) { success() } else { status.textContent = 'An error occurred. Please try again.'; console.debug('HTTP:', res.status); }`
findings.evidence: Error message line 407: `status.textContent = \`Unexpected error (${res.status}). Please try again.\`;` Shows HTTP status code to user. While helpful, it reveals system details.
findings.impact: Information disclosure vulnerability. ISO 27001 A.8.26 recommends limiting user-facing error messages. Low risk on its own, but in combination with other vulnerabilities, could aid an attacker in reconnaissance.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.26 - Logging & Monitoring Best Practice

findings.title: No self-service account deletion mechanism
findings.description: Privacy policy states users can delete accounts but settings page provides no deletion UI. Users must request deletion via email.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add account deletion button to settings page with double-confirmation and cascade deletion of all user data.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy mentions deletion but no UI exists in settings page.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 17 requires easy account deletion. Non-compliance could trigger fines.

findings.title: CORS headers reflect untrusted origin without validation
findings.description: Lambda API reflects incoming Origin header directly to CORS response, enabling CSRF attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement origin whitelist: validate Origin against allowed domains before returning in CORS header.
findings.evidence: corsHeaders() returns Origin without whitelist validation.
findings.impact: CORS misconfiguration enables CSRF attacks on authenticated users.

findings.title: No multi-factor authentication (MFA) implementation
findings.description: Application relies solely on email and password. No TOTP, SMS, or security key support.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement TOTP-based MFA with recovery codes for account recovery.
findings.evidence: Login only checks email/password. No MFA prompts or validation.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 recommends MFA. Account compromise increases breach risk.

findings.title: No explicit cookie consent banner or preference center
findings.description: Plausible Analytics is integrated but no cookie consent mechanism exists. GDPR requires opt-in consent before tracking.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add cookie consent banner disclosing essential vs. analytics cookies. Load Plausible only after user consents.
findings.evidence: Analytics script included without consent banner or preference UI.
findings.impact: GDPR requires opt-in for non-essential cookies. Regulators fine €20k-€50k+ for non-compliant tracking.

findings.title: Authentication tokens stored in localStorage without HttpOnly flag
findings.description: Bearer tokens stored in localStorage, accessible to JavaScript. XSS attacks can steal tokens.
findings.fix_suggestion: Migrate to HttpOnly, Secure cookies with SameSite=Strict. Requires backend API changes.
findings.evidence: Tokens stored in localStorage: localStorage.setItem(TOKEN_KEY, token)
findings.impact: ISO 27001 recommends HttpOnly cookies. XSS can compromise authenticated sessions.

findings.title: Missing granular consent for Claude API / AI processing
findings.description: Claude API integration exists but no user consent mechanism or disclosure of current usage.
findings.fix_suggestion: Disclose current Claude API usage in privacy policy. Add settings toggle to opt out of AI features.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy mentions AI 'in future' but current usage and consent mechanism undefined.
findings.impact: GDPR requires informed consent for AI processing. Undisclosed AI usage violates Art. 13.
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 13, Art. 25 (Data Protection by Design)

findings.title: Data export does not include all personal data
findings.description: Export only includes local app data (journals, sessions). Server-side account data, events, and attendance not included.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement /api/account/export endpoint retrieving all user data (profile, events, attendees, QR scans) in JSON/CSV.
findings.evidence: buildLocalDataExport() only exports localStorage data, not server-side profile, events, or attendee records.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 20 requires complete personal data export in machine-readable format.

findings.title: No documented session timeout or inactivity lockout
findings.description: Sessions remain active indefinitely until manual logout. No inactivity timeout.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement client-side activity tracking with 30-minute inactivity timeout and warning.
findings.evidence: logout() clears token but no automatic timeout on inactivity.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.4 recommends session timeout. Unattended devices at risk of unauthorized access.

findings.title: No documented Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)
findings.description: Processing personal data of event organizers, attendees, speakers without documented risk assessment.
findings.fix_suggestion: Conduct and document DPIA covering: purpose, data categories, recipients, risks, and mitigation measures.
findings.evidence: No DPIA document found. Privacy policy lacks formal risk assessment.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 35 requires DPIA for high-risk processing. Absence during audit is a procedural gap.

findings.title: Content Security Policy allows unsafe-eval
findings.description: CSP includes 'script-src unsafe-eval' which weakens XSS defense.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove 'unsafe-eval' from CSP. Test for functionality breaks.
findings.evidence: CSP: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval'
findings.impact: unsafe-eval weakens XSS defenses. Modern React doesn't require it.

findings.title: No age verification or minor data protections
findings.description: Privacy policy states 16+ but no age gate. Organizers can add children as attendees without age verification.
