findings.fix_suggestion: Add optional 'creating events for minors' flag. Implement privacy-by-default for child-focused events.
findings.evidence: No age verification in registration. No parental consent mechanism for minor attendees.
findings.impact: If service is 'likely' to be used for children, COPPA/AADC apply. Non-compliance triggers warnings.

findings.title: No visible client-side error/audit logging disclosure
findings.description: Privacy policy mentions logs but no detailed retention policy or user audit log access.
findings.fix_suggestion: Document logging policy in privacy statement. Provide API/UI for users to request audit logs.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy vaguely mentions 'operational logs ~30 days' without detail.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 13 requires transparency about logging. Users should know what is logged and how long.

findings.title: CSP allows unsafe-inline for scripts
findings.description: CSP includes 'script-src unsafe-inline' which weakens XSS defense (though less critical than unsafe-eval).
findings.fix_suggestion: Attempt to remove unsafe-inline by avoiding inline event handlers and styles.
findings.evidence: CSP: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'
findings.impact: Low—React often requires unsafe-inline. Not a major vulnerability.

findings.title: Privacy policy lacks detail on event data retention
findings.description: Retention policy for guest lists, QR scans, gallery uploads is unclear.
findings.fix_suggestion: Update privacy policy with event data retention schedule (e.g., deleted when event deleted).
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states 'data remains until deletion' but event-specific retention undefined.
findings.impact: Low—vague retention policies are common observations but not violations if deletion works.

findings.title: Password hashing algorithm not disclosed
findings.description: Privacy policy mentions 'hashed password' but does not specify algorithm (bcrypt, argon2, etc.).
findings.fix_suggestion: Disclose password hashing algorithm in privacy policy or security page.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy: 'hashed password' with no algorithm specified.
findings.impact: Low—transparency issue, not a violation. But users cannot assess security.

findings.title: Privacy policy does not mention Anthropic DPA
findings.description: Claude API integration not fully disclosed. No mention of Anthropic as data processor or DPA.
findings.fix_suggestion: Update privacy policy to disclose: 'AI processing via Anthropic Claude API, bound by DPA.' Verify DPA exists.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy mentions AI but not Anthropic by name or DPA status.
findings.impact: Low—if AI features are unused. Before activation, DPA required.

findings.title: Encryption method for backups not disclosed
findings.description: Encryption of backed-up journal entries uses AES-256-GCM but method not disclosed to users.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add note: 'Backups encrypted with AES-256-GCM; key derived via PBKDF2.'
findings.evidence: Code uses AES-256-GCM with PBKDF2 600k iterations but privacy policy doesn't mention.
findings.impact: Low—strong encryption implemented. Transparency is nice-to-have.

findings.title: No mechanism to object to marketing/profiling
findings.description: Privacy policy mentions 'right to object' but no unsubscribe UI or marketing preference controls.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add 'Communication Preferences' to settings with toggle for marketing emails.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy claims right to object. No settings UI for marketing opt-out.
findings.impact: Low if marketing emails not sent. But CAN-SPAM requires unsubscribe link.

findings.title: DynamoDB auto-scaling without documented rate limiting
findings.description: DynamoDB on-demand billing means costs scale with usage. No API rate limiting visible.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement API rate limiting (e.g., 100 req/min per user). Monitor costs.
findings.evidence: DynamoDB: billing_mode = PAY_PER_REQUEST. No rate limiting in API.
findings.impact: Low—operational/cost issue. But API abuse (economic DoS) possible.

findings.title: Account Deletion API Endpoint Not Exposed in UI
findings.description: The API endpoint /api/account (DELETE) implements the GDPR right to erasure by deleting all user data, but the UI in settings/page.tsx does not expose a 'Delete Account' button. Users cannot exercise their right to erasure without direct API access, violating GDPR Art. 17 requirement that erasure requests must be possible through the service interface.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a 'Delete Account' section in the Settings page with password confirmation dialog, similar to the Export Data button. The confirmation should clearly explain this is irreversible. Implement handleDeleteAccount in the settings page that calls POST /api/account with the user's password.
findings.evidence: The settings page exports/import handleExport and handleDeleteWeight but does not implement handleDeleteAccount. The API handler at lambda/api/index.mjs:2095 fully implements deletion.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 17 violation. Regulatory fines up to €20 million or 4% annual revenue. Users unable to exercise legal rights could trigger complaints to supervisory authorities.

findings.title: Missing Data Retention Policy Disclosure
findings.description: The privacy policy discloses what data is collected and how it's stored, but does not explicitly state how long data is retained. Article states 'Your data is retained for as long as your account exists' but provides no guidance on automated deletion, archival, or regulatory retention timelines. This violates GDPR Art. 13(2)(a) requirement to inform users of retention periods.
findings.fix_suggestion: Expand the privacy policy to explicitly state: (1) retention period for each data category (e.g., 'Nutrition logs retained for account lifetime, deleted immediately on account deletion'), (2) backup and archive retention periods (e.g., 'Backups retained for 30 days after deletion'), (3) logs retention policy (e.g., 'API logs retained 30 days, CloudFront logs 90 days'), (4) any regulatory retention requirements if applicable.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 'Data Retention & Deletion' (lines 165-177) states data is retained indefinitely during account lifetime, but does not specify if/when deleted data is purged from backups, archives, or logs.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 13 transparency violation. Inadequate information to users about data lifecycle. Potential regulatory action by supervisory authorities and inability to demonstrate compliance with storage limitation principle (Art. 5(1)(e)).

findings.title: No Data Protection Officer Contact Information
findings.description: The privacy policy provides a contact email (noreply@codyjo.com) for privacy inquiries but does not identify whether a Data Protection Officer (DPO) exists, and if so, does not provide their direct contact. GDPR Art. 13(2)(b) and Art. 37 require organizations subject to mandatory DPO requirements to publish DPO contact details.
findings.fix_suggestion: Clarify in the privacy policy whether Cody Jo Method has appointed a Data Protection Officer. If yes, provide DPO email/contact. If no, add a statement: 'We have not appointed a dedicated DPO as we do not meet GDPR DPO appointment triggers. For privacy matters, contact privacy@fuel.codyjo.com.' Ensure the designated privacy contact is monitored and responsive.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lists only general support email (noreply@codyjo.com). No reference to a DPO or privacy team contact.
findings.impact: If subject to DPO requirements (data processing at scale, public body, systematic monitoring), absence of DPO contact is a GDPR violation (Art. 37(6)). Supervisory authorities routinely check for DPO contact availability. Potential enforcement action and fines.

findings.title: No Right to Object to Direct Marketing (if applicable)
findings.description: The privacy policy does not mention direct marketing, newsletter subscriptions, or a mechanism for users to object to direct marketing processing. If email marketing is planned, there must be an explicit right to object per GDPR Art. 21(2).
findings.fix_suggestion: If email marketing is not planned, no action needed. If marketing is planned: (1) Update privacy policy to disclose marketing email processing and lawful basis (explicit opt-in consent), (2) Implement preference center allowing users to unsubscribe from marketing, (3) Ensure all marketing emails include unsubscribe link per CAN-SPAM/GDPR.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy does not mention email marketing, promotional communications, or unsubscribe mechanisms. Resend integration is mentioned for transactional emails only (password reset, verification), but no mention of marketing email capability.
findings.impact: If direct marketing processing is conducted without proper consent and right-to-object mechanism, GDPR Art. 21 violation. Potential enforcement action, particularly in EU jurisdictions with strict ePrivacy Directive requirements.
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 6 & 21 (Lawful Basis & Right to Object)

findings.title: No Documented Breach Notification Process
findings.description: The README mentions security headers and encryption but does not document incident response procedures, breach notification timelines, or communication templates. GDPR requires breach notification within 72 hours to supervisory authorities and to affected individuals without undue delay. ISO 27001 A.16 requires incident management procedures.
findings.fix_suggestion: Create an incident response runbook documenting: (1) Detection mechanisms (CloudWatch alerts, security event monitoring), (2) Notification templates for supervisory authorities and affected users, (3) Escalation procedures (e.g., escalate to management within 1 hour of detection), (4) 72-hour reporting deadline for GDPR, (5) Post-incident review process. Implement automated alerting for suspicious activities in CloudWatch.
findings.evidence: No documented incident response procedures in code or infrastructure documentation. No evidence of breach notification templates, escalation procedures, or automated alerting for security events.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 33-34 non-compliance if breaches cannot be reported within 72 hours. Supervisory authorities expect incident response documentation. Lack of procedure increases response time and legal exposure.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.5 & A.14.1 (Access Control & Incident Management)

findings.title: No Documented Session Timeout or Inactivity Limits
findings.description: JWT tokens are issued with 30-day expiry, but there is no documented session timeout for inactivity. Users accessing sensitive health data could remain authenticated indefinitely without activity, increasing exposure if a device is lost or stolen. ISO 27001 A.9.4.3 requires session timeout controls.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement idle session timeout: (1) On frontend, track user activity (mouse, keyboard, touch), (2) If inactive for 15-30 minutes, prompt user to confirm activity or auto-logout, (3) Send reminder notification before logout, (4) Clear tokens and cached data on logout. Document the policy in security standards.
findings.evidence: JWT creation at line 162 shows 30-day absolute expiry (86400 * 30), but no idle/inactivity timeout mechanism. Frontend does not show signs of logout on inactivity.
findings.impact: Weak session management increases risk of unauthorized access to health data after device loss/theft. Not directly a GDPR violation but demonstrates weak information security controls under ISO 27001 / Art. 32.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.1 & A.9.4 (User Access Management & Session Management)

findings.title: CSP Allows 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval'
findings.description: The Content Security Policy in CloudFront (terraform/cloudfront.tf:218) includes 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' in script-src, significantly weakening XSS protections. Modern best practice is to eliminate these except where absolutely necessary, and instead use nonces or strict CSP.
findings.fix_suggestion: Refactor to eliminate unsafe directives: (1) Extract all inline scripts to external files with nonces, (2) Avoid dynamic eval() calls, (3) Use Content Security Policy Level 3 with nonce-based script execution, (4) Use inline styles via CSS classes instead of style attributes, (5) Update CSP to: "script-src 'self' 'nonce-XXXX' https://analytics.codyjo.com; style-src 'self' 'nonce-XXXX'". This requires refactoring layout.tsx inline scripts (lines 51-53).
findings.evidence: CSP policy: "script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' https://analytics.codyjo.com" — the 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' directives allow any inline scripts and dynamic code execution, undermining CSP's XSS protection.
findings.impact: Increased XSS vulnerability risk. While not a direct GDPR violation, weak XSS protection jeopardizes confidentiality and integrity of user health data, violating GDPR Art. 32 (security measures). Could facilitate data exfiltration or session hijacking.

findings.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) Documentation
findings.description: The system processes sensitive health data including medication records, body composition, and wellness information. GDPR Art. 35 requires a DPIA for processing that is likely to result in high risk, particularly systematic monitoring or large-scale processing. No evidence of DPIA documentation.
findings.fix_suggestion: Conduct a DPIA documenting: (1) Purpose and necessity of health data processing, (2) Risk assessment for confidentiality/integrity breaches, (3) Mitigation measures (encryption, access controls, retention limits), (4) Residual risk assessment, (5) Consultation with supervisory authority if high risk remains. Document and retain for audit purposes.
findings.evidence: README and CLAUDE.md do not reference any DPIA process, documentation, or risk assessment. The system processes sensitive health/medical data, which typically triggers DPIA requirements.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 35 non-compliance. Supervisory authorities expect DPIA documentation for health data processing. Lack of DPIA may indicate failure to identify and mitigate privacy risks, leading to enforcement action.

findings.title: API Keys Stored in Lambda Environment Variables
findings.description: The Anthropic API key and USDA API key are stored in Lambda environment variables (README.md:351), not in AWS Secrets Manager. While Secrets Manager is used for JWT_SECRET, external API keys should also use Secrets Manager for rotation and audit trail benefits.
findings.fix_suggestion: Move external API keys to Secrets Manager: (1) Store ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and USDA_API_KEY in Secrets Manager (not env vars), (2) Update Lambda to fetch from Secrets Manager like JWT_SECRET, (3) Implement key rotation policy with Secrets Manager automatic rotation, (4) Audit key access via CloudTrail.
findings.evidence: Environment variables documented: 'ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: Lambda env' and 'USDA_API_KEY: Lambda env'. These keys are visible in Lambda console and CloudWatch logs if logging is verbose.
findings.impact: If keys are exposed in logs or console, unauthorized API usage could occur, potentially incurring costs or accessing user data through Anthropic. Not a direct GDPR violation but weak secrets management under ISO 27001 A.8.24.

findings.title: Limited Audit Trail for Data Access/Modification
findings.description: API logs (30-day retention) capture requests but the privacy policy and code do not document comprehensive audit trails for sensitive data access (e.g., who accessed whose medication records, when wellness data was exported). ISO 27001 A.12.4 requires audit trails for security-relevant events.
findings.fix_suggestion: Enhance audit logging: (1) Log all data access with user ID, endpoint, data category (food/medication/wellness), and result code, (2) Log all data modifications (PUT/DELETE) with old and new values (or hashes for large fields), (3) Retain logs for minimum 1 year, (4) Implement CloudWatch log insights queries for data access audits, (5) Document in privacy policy: 'Data access is logged for security and compliance purposes and retained for 1 year.'
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states 'API request logs are retained for 30 days and include request timestamps, HTTP methods, and response status codes' but does not mention logging of user IDs, data categories accessed, or modification timestamps for individual records.
findings.impact: Insufficient audit trail makes it difficult to demonstrate GDPR compliance in case of incident, and limits ability to detect unauthorized access. Not a direct violation but weakness in Art. 32 security controls.

findings.title: Password Requirement Discrepancy Between Backend and Frontend
findings.description: The backend requires passwords to be at least 10 characters (lambda/api/index.mjs:422), but the frontend validation message says 'At least 8 characters' (register/page.tsx:118). This discrepancy could confuse users and create inconsistent UX.
findings.fix_suggestion: Update frontend validation message to match backend requirement. Change register/page.tsx:118 placeholder and error messages from 'At least 8 characters' to 'At least 10 characters'. Verify login page doesn't have similar discrepancies.
findings.evidence: Frontend placeholder text: 'At least 8 characters' but backend check at lambda/api/index.mjs:422 requires: 'Password must be at least 10 characters'.
findings.impact: Not a compliance violation but a usability issue. Could lead to user confusion and support burden. Minor data quality issue.

findings.title: Missing Cookie Consent Banner
findings.description: No explicit cookie consent implementation. While Plausible is cookieless by design, GDPR best practice requires users to opt-in to analytics before tracking begins. The absence of a consent banner may violate GDPR Article 7 (conditions for consent) and Article 13 (information to be provided).
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement cookie consent banner (e.g., CookieBot, OneTrust, or custom) that: (1) obtains explicit opt-in before Plausible tracking; (2) discloses third-party cookies; (3) provides easy opt-out; (4) respects Do Not Track header.
findings.impact: Regulatory fines up to €20M or 4% of global revenue; user complaints to DPA; non-compliance with EDPB guidelines on cookie consent.

findings.title: No Explicit Lawful Basis for Account Activity Tracking
findings.description: Privacy policy discloses tracking (Plausible, Anthropic API calls) but does not explicitly state which lawful basis applies to activity monitoring. GDPR requires one of six bases (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, legitimate interest) for each processing activity. Current language is vague on whether tracking is contractual, consent-based, or legitimate interest.
findings.fix_suggestion: Update privacy policy to explicitly state: '(1) Plausible analytics relies on legitimate interest (app improvement); (2) API call logging relies on contract performance; (3) Account activity tracking for fraud prevention relies on legitimate interest.' Include legitimacy assessment balancing.
findings.impact: Processing deemed unlawful; user rights violations; DPA enforcement action; potential court injunctions to cease tracking.

findings.title: Missing DPIA Documentation Reference
findings.description: No evidence of Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) documentation for high-risk processing activities: (1) E2E encryption key storage and journal encryption; (2) Third-party API integrations (Anthropic, Bolls.life, Resend); (3) User behavior tracking (Plausible); (4) Cross-border data transfers. GDPR requires DPIA for processing using new technologies or large-scale processing.
findings.fix_suggestion: Conduct and document DPIA covering: (1) Purpose and necessity of tracking activities; (2) Risk assessment for E2E encryption implementation; (3) Third-party processor agreements and risk analysis; (4) Mitigation measures (e.g., data minimization, access controls). Store DPIA document in compliant location and update privacy policy to reference it.
findings.impact: Non-compliance with mandatory DPIA requirement; regulatory fines; inability to demonstrate risk mitigation to DPA; potential processing suspension.

findings.title: Account Deletion Does Not Cover Third-Party Data Retention
findings.description: User account deletion removes local data (studies, journals, bookmarks) but privacy policy does not clearly explain retention in third-party systems: (1) Resend email logs (sending confirmation/password reset emails) may retain data for compliance/spam prevention; (2) Anthropic API call history retained for 30 days; (3) Bolls.life does not delete user query history. Incomplete data erasure violates GDPR Article 17.
findings.fix_suggestion: Update privacy policy with: (1) 'Resend email logs are retained for X days for spam/abuse prevention; we request deletion but cannot guarantee immediate removal'; (2) 'Anthropic API call history is retained for 30 days per their terms; we do not control deletion'; (3) 'Bolls.life does not delete query history.' Add account deletion endpoint response that acknowledges third-party retention periods. Consider adding Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with Anthropic and Resend to ensure right to erasure.
findings.impact: Right to be forgotten violation; user data persists in third-party systems; DPA enforcement; potential fines for incomplete erasure.

findings.title: No API Export for Synced Cloud Data
findings.description: App offers local data export (via IndexedDB) but no comprehensive API endpoint to export all user data in structured format. Right to data portability requires users to obtain a copy of personal data they've provided in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format (e.g., CSV, JSON). Missing export for: studies, journals, bookmarks, reading history from DynamoDB.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement GET /api/data-export endpoint that: (1) Returns ZIP containing JSON exports of studies, journals, bookmarks, reading history; (2) Includes metadata (creation date, modification date, encryption status); (3) Sends via email or direct download; (4) Logs data portability request for audit trail. Add UI button in account settings to trigger export.
findings.impact: Right to data portability violation; users cannot switch to competitor easily; DPA enforcement action; potential fines.

findings.title: CSP Includes 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' for script-src
findings.description: Content Security Policy in meta tag allows 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' for script sources. This permits arbitrary inline JavaScript execution, defeating XSS protection. Attackers could inject malicious scripts via DOM manipulation or developer console. 'unsafe-eval' additionally allows dynamic code execution (eval(), Function(), setTimeout/setInterval with strings).
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' from script-src. Instead: (1) Use Content Security Policy nonce or hash for inline scripts; (2) Move inline styles/scripts to external files; (3) Use external script loading without dynamic evaluation; (4) Implement Subresource Integrity (SRI) for CDN resources. Update CSP to: script-src 'nonce-<random>' 'strict-dynamic' https:; style-src 'nonce-<random>' https:;
findings.impact: Cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities; data theft; malware injection; compliance violations with security standards; breach of ISO 27001 A.12.2.1.

findings.title: Password Minimum Length Below NIST Recommendations
findings.description: Password minimum is 10 characters. NIST Special Publication 800-63B recommends minimum 12 characters (or 8+ with complexity rules) for high-security accounts handling personal data. 10-character passwords are crackable with modern GPU attacks in reasonable time (days/weeks).
findings.fix_suggestion: Increase password minimum to 12 characters OR implement complexity rules (uppercase, lowercase, number, special char) to enable 8-character minimum. Recommendation: 12-character minimum without complexity (easier for users, stronger in practice).
findings.impact: Weak password enforcement; increased account compromise risk; potential breach of user data; compliance gap with NIST 800-63B security standards.

findings.title: No Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Support
findings.description: App uses password-only authentication. No MFA option (TOTP, SMS, email confirmation on login) for accounts containing sensitive user data (journals, personal Bible study notes). Single factor authentication is insufficient for ISO 27001 baseline and NIST guidelines for high-value targets.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement optional MFA: (1) TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) via Google Authenticator/Authy - lowest friction; (2) Email confirmation on new device login (implicit MFA); (3) SMS OTP (optional, requires SMS provider integration). Start with TOTP (small effort), add email MFA next (medium effort).
findings.impact: Account takeover vulnerability; unauthorized access to personal journals; data breach risk; non-compliance with security standards; potential regulatory scrutiny.

findings.title: No Rate Limiting on Non-Auth API Endpoints
findings.description: Rate limiting is implemented for auth endpoints (registration 3/hr, login 5/15min) but not for general API endpoints (journals, studies, bookmarks). Missing rate limits on: GET /api/journals, POST /journals, DELETE /journals, GET /studies, POST /studies. Attackers could enumerate resources or cause DoS without detection.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement global API rate limiting: (1) Per-user limit: 100 requests/minute for authenticated users; (2) Per-IP limit: 1000 requests/minute for all traffic; (3) Endpoint-specific limits for expensive operations (e.g., journal encryption: 10/minute). Use middleware (rate-limiter-flexible, express-rate-limit) and return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header.
findings.impact: Resource exhaustion attacks; enumeration of user data; DoS vulnerability; increased database costs; non-compliance with monitoring requirements.

findings.title: No Documented CORS Configuration or Origin Whitelist
findings.description: Backend sends CORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin) but no documented approach to validate origins. No visible code enforcing strict origin whitelist. Could permit unauthorized cross-origin requests if misconfigured.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement explicit CORS whitelist: (1) Define approved origins in environment variable: CORS_ORIGINS=https://selahscripture.com,https://app.selahscripture.com; (2) Validate Origin header against whitelist on every request; (3) Return 403 Forbidden for non-whitelisted origins; (4) Document in README. Use express-cors with credentials: true and origin function.
findings.impact: Cross-Origin Request Forgery (CORS vulnerability); unauthorized API access; data theft from JavaScript running on compromised domains.

findings.title: No Documented Log Encryption or Access Controls
findings.description: CloudWatch logs contain sensitive information (auth events, account deletion, encryption key setup) but no evidence of: (1) log encryption at rest; (2) log access controls (IAM policies); (3) retention policies; (4) log integrity verification. AWS Lambda logs are readable by any AWS account user with CloudWatch permissions.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement log security: (1) Enable CloudWatch log encryption (AWS KMS key); (2) Restrict CloudWatch access via IAM policy (only security team); (3) Set retention policy to 90 days (NIST baseline); (4) Archive logs to S3 (immutable) for long-term storage; (5) Enable S3 Object Lock for tamper-proof storage. Document in security runbook.
findings.impact: Unauthorized access to audit logs; log tampering; non-compliance with log security requirements; inability to prove audit trail integrity.

findings.title: No Documented Incident Response Runbook or Breach Notification Procedure
findings.description: App handles sensitive user data (journals, personal notes) but no documented incident response plan. Missing: (1) breach detection procedures; (2) containment steps; (3) notification timeline (GDPR requires 72-hour notification to DPA); (4) communication templates for users; (5) forensics/investigation procedures. Critical for compliance with GDPR Article 33 and ISO 27001 A.16.
findings.fix_suggestion: Create and document incident response runbook: (1) Detection & triage (monitor CloudWatch for suspicious activity); (2) Containment (revoke sessions, isolate affected accounts); (3) Forensics (preserve logs, identify scope); (4) Internal notification (CEO, legal, security team within 24h); (5) User notification (breach notification email template); (6) DPA notification (prepare GDPR breach notification); (7) Post-incident review (RCA). Store runbook in shared secure location and conduct tabletop exercise quarterly.
findings.impact: Inability to respond to breaches promptly; GDPR breach notification violations (72-hour requirement); regulatory fines; user lawsuits; reputational damage.

findings.title: API Request Size Limit Not Uniformly Enforced
findings.description: Journal endpoint returns 413 for payloads >200KB, but other endpoints lack explicit size limits. Missing limits could allow: (1) large payload DoS attacks; (2) memory exhaustion; (3) database overload. Should enforce consistently across all endpoints accepting user input.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement global request size limit middleware: (1) Limit all JSON payloads to 100KB (express.json({limit: '100kb'})); (2) Limit file uploads to 10MB; (3) Document limits in API spec. Customize per-endpoint if needed (e.g., journal encryption data may need higher limit).
findings.impact: DoS vulnerability; resource exhaustion; service unavailability; increased operational costs.

findings.title: Age Verification Relies on Self-Attestation Only
findings.description: Registration form includes checkbox 'I confirm I am at least 16 years old' but no technical verification. Self-attestation is easily bypassed by users under 16. No parental consent flow for minors 13-15. No age-gating documented in privacy policy beyond text disclosure. Cannot verify actual user age.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement tiered age verification: (1) Lightweight: ID document verification (Veriff, Socure, Jumio) for users claiming 16+; (2) Parental consent: For users unable to verify age, require parental email consent with one-click confirmation; (3) Access restrictions: Block account if age verification fails; (4) Documentation: Add 'Age Verification & Parental Consent Policy' to privacy policy. Start with self-attestation + parental consent for non-verified users.
findings.impact: COPPA violations if accessed by <13 users; UK Online Safety Act enforcement; California age-appropriate design law compliance risk; potential age discrimination claims; regulatory scrutiny for child safety.
findings.regulation: COPPA, UK Online Safety Act, California Age-Appropriate Design Code

findings.title: No Age-Appropriate Design Review Documentation
findings.description: Privacy policy and documentation do not mention age-appropriate design principles (AADC, EDSA, OSA compliance). No evidence of: (1) dark pattern avoidance; (2) nudging transparency; (3) data minimization for minors; (4) parental controls; (5) child safety by design audit. Required by California AADC and UK Online Safety Act if accessed by minors.
findings.fix_suggestion: Conduct age-appropriate design review: (1) Audit UI for dark patterns (urgency, social proof, infinite scroll); (2) Ensure data minimization (collect minimum necessary); (3) Add parental controls (access restrictions, content filtering); (4) Document design principles in privacy policy & AADC compliance section; (5) Conduct user testing with teens 13-16; (6) Consider hiring AADC consultant (e.g., Common Sense Media). Implement findings.
findings.impact: California age-appropriate design law violations (fines up to $7,500 per violation); UK Online Safety Act enforcement; potential state AG action; reputational damage if minors are harmed.
findings.regulation: UK Online Safety Act, California AADC, European Digital Services Act

findings.title: No Data Minimization Policy for Minor Users
findings.description: If minors access the app (despite 16+ policy), no special data protection applies. Privacy policy lacks: (1) special protection for minor user data; (2) shorter retention periods for minors; (3) parental access to child data; (4) restrictions on data sharing with third parties (Anthropic AI, Bolls.life). GDPR Article 8 and COPPA require enhanced protection for child data.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add privacy policy section: 'Protection of Minor Data': (1) If we discover user is under 16, account is suspended pending parental consent; (2) Minor data is deleted after 30 days if no parental consent provided; (3) Parent can request access to minor's data; (4) Parent can delete child's account; (5) Minimal third-party sharing for minors (e.g., Anthropic API calls anonymized); (6) Shorter retention (30 days vs 90 days for adults). Implement backend enforcement.
findings.impact: GDPR Article 8 violation for <18 users; COPPA violations for <13; potential fines and enforcement; user lawsuits.

findings.title: No Geographic-Aware Age Verification or Enforcement
findings.description: App does not detect or enforce different age/privacy requirements by geography. UK OSA may require different protections for UK users <18. California CCPA/AADC may apply to California residents. EU GDPR Chapter III applies to EU users. No IP-based geolocation or self-reported location to trigger region-specific rules.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement geographic-aware enforcement: (1) Detect user location via IP geolocation (GeoIP2, MaxMind) at signup; (2) Store location in user profile; (3) Apply region-specific rules: UK users <18 require additional consent; CA residents get CCPA deletion rights; EU users get GDPR rights; (4) Display localized privacy policies; (5) Document geo-enforcement in privacy policy.
findings.impact: Non-compliance with regional laws; inability to enforce different requirements by jurisdiction; regulatory action in multiple jurisdictions.
findings.regulation: UK Online Safety Act, California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA)

findings.title: Missing Consent Withdrawal Mechanism
findings.description: Privacy policy does not document how users can withdraw consent for analytics (Plausible) or other consent-based processing. Users should have easy one-click withdrawal without account deletion.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add account settings section: 'Privacy Preferences': (1) Checkbox to opt-out of Plausible analytics; (2) Checkbox to opt-out of marketing emails; (3) Save preference to user profile; (4) Update privacy policy with withdrawal instructions.
findings.impact: Incomplete consent mechanisms; user inability to exercise data rights; GDPR Article 7 non-compliance.

findings.title: No Documented API Security Policy or Standards
findings.description: API lacks documented security standards: (1) no documented authentication method in API spec; (2) no token expiry explanation; (3) no rate-limit documentation; (4) no error handling guidance (e.g., avoid exposing stack traces). Makes it difficult for developers to follow security practices.
findings.fix_suggestion: Create API Security Policy document: (1) Authentication (Bearer token, expiry 30 days); (2) Rate limits (per endpoint); (3) HTTPS requirement; (4) Error handling (no stack traces, generic 500 errors); (5) Logging (what gets logged, retention); (6) Data minimization (required fields only). Add to API documentation (README, OpenAPI spec).
findings.impact: Misconfiguration risk; developer security mistakes; non-compliance with security policy documentation requirements.

findings.title: No data retention policy for personal data
findings.description: The privacy policy states operational logs are retained for about 30 days but provides no explicit retention periods for account data. GDPR requires data is kept only as long as necessary. Without documented retention policies, users cannot understand data storage duration.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Document explicit retention periods per data category. 2. Update privacy policy. 3. Implement automated deletion (DynamoDB TTL, scheduled Lambda). 4. Add user-configurable retention settings.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy: 'Operational logs generally retained about 30 days unless needed longer for security review.' No TTL for user account data, readings, journals.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) and 6 violations. Fines up to €20M or 4% annual turnover.

findings.title: Incomplete privacy policy for third-party data processing
findings.description: Privacy discloses Anthropic processes reading context but lacks detail on legal basis, DPA status, data transfers, or user control. Plausible analytics mentioned but no granular consent mechanism.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Detail Anthropic as Data Processor with DPA link. 2. Clarify lawful basis and feature necessity. 3. Document data transfers/adequacy decisions. 4. Add explicit analytics consent (opt-in). 5. Link to Anthropic privacy policy.
findings.evidence: Privacy: 'reading context may be processed by Anthropic.' Missing: DPA status, transfer mechanisms, Anthropic's role, user control.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 13(1)(c) and 14(1)(c) violations. Regulatory enforcement, reputational damage.

findings.title: No privacy policy re-consent on updates
findings.description: Policy version tracked but no mechanism requires re-consent if policy updates. Users continue using service indefinitely after policy changes without re-accepting.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Check policy version on login; flag as 'policy_update_pending' if older. 2. Show modal requiring re-acceptance before features. 3. Track re-consent timestamp/version. 4. Implement granular consent management. 5. Document policy versioning.
findings.evidence: Registration stores privacyPolicyVersion but login doesn't check current version. No forced re-acceptance on policy updates.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 7(4) and Art. 12-14 violations. Material policy changes require informed re-consent.

findings.title: Missing consent for non-essential analytics
findings.description: Plausible analytics script loads unconditionally without consent banner. While cookieless, GDPR requires prior explicit consent for non-essential processing.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Implement consent banner. 2. Separate essential vs. non-essential consent. 3. Load Plausible only after opt-in. 4. Store consent in localStorage. 5. Use opt-in model before analytics.
findings.evidence: Script loads unconditionally: <script async src='https://analytics.codyjo.com/js/pa-RUx1crBjD6Rrg5MResyha.js' />
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 5(1)(a) and Art. 7 violations. Fines up to €20M or 4% turnover.

findings.title: Account deletion doesn't fully purge shared reading tokens
findings.description: Account deletion deletes USER# records but not SHARE# tokens. Shared reading metadata exists with 90-day TTL but incomplete erasure violates right to be forgotten.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Query SHARE# tokens by userId and delete explicitly. 2. Delete SHARE# when reading deleted. 3. Document TTL as secondary mechanism. 4. Confirm all data deleted message.
findings.evidence: handleDeleteAccount deletes USER# items but not SHARE# tokens. Shares created with 90-day TTL; not cleaned if reading deleted.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 17 violation. Residual SHARE# tokens could allow deleted reading access.

findings.title: Limited ability to rectify inaccurate data
findings.description: Users can update profile but cannot correct reading/journal data after creation. No audit trail of corrections.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Allow edit of readings (question, notes), journals, daily cards. 2. Maintain immutable audit log. 3. Provide correction log in export. 4. Update privacy policy on data corrections.
findings.evidence: Settings allows name/email/password changes; readings/journals immutable after creation.
findings.impact: Users cannot correct inaccurate data. Limited recourse without delete/recreate.

findings.title: No opt-out mechanism for marketing/profiling
findings.description: No documented process for objecting to direct marketing or profiling. Privacy mentions 'right to object' but no mechanism.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Document objection contact (cody@codyjo.com). 2. Add unsubscribe in marketing emails. 3. Implement /api/preferences. 4. Document objection process.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy mentions right to object but no mechanism or email provided.
findings.impact: If marketing/profiling added, violates Art. 21.

findings.title: No documented backup and disaster recovery
findings.description: DynamoDB PITR enabled but no RTO/RPO/breach notification procedures documented.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Document RTO/RPO and backup testing. 2. Create incident response plan. 3. Define reportable breach criteria. 4. Designate breach response lead. 5. Test quarterly.
findings.evidence: PITR enabled but no documented recovery procedures or breach notification process.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 5(1)(f) and Art. 33-34 violations. Cannot meet 72-hour breach notification.

findings.title: Privacy policy doesn't disclose shared reading link expiry
findings.description: App allows 90-day-expiring shared reading links but privacy policy doesn't mention feature, expiry, or public accessibility.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Add sharing section to privacy policy. 2. Warn users before share generation. 3. Allow immediate revocation instead of waiting for TTL.
findings.evidence: No mention of sharing feature, 90-day expiry, or public link accessibility.
findings.impact: Lack of transparency about sharing. Users may unintentionally share data.

findings.title: CSP allows unsafe-inline for scripts
findings.description: CSP includes 'unsafe-inline' for script-src, weakening XSS protection.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Remove 'unsafe-inline'. 2. Use nonce-based CSP. 3. Restrict to external domains only. 4. Test app functionality.
findings.evidence: CSP: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://analytics.codyjo.com
findings.impact: Allows DOM-based XSS if unfiltered input in inline scripts.

findings.title: Admin allowlist cached 5 minutes; access retained after removal
findings.description: Admin allowlist cached in Lambda for 5 minutes. Removed admins retain access during cache window.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Reduce cache to 30-60 seconds. 2. Implement cache invalidation on secret update. 3. Log revocations. 4. Document known risk.
findings.evidence: ADMIN_CACHE_TTL = 5 * 60 * 1000. Removed admin can access for up to 5 minutes.
findings.impact: Privilege escalation window. Should lose access immediately.

findings.title: 6-digit password reset code vulnerable to brute force
findings.description: Password reset codes use only 6 digits (100000-999999), ~900k values. Rate limit is per-token but token space small.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Use 8+ digits or alphanumeric (16+ chars). 2. Implement per-email rate limiting. 3. Add exponential backoff. 4. Consider TOTP or magic links.
findings.evidence: generateCode() returns 6-digit number. Reset limited 5 attempts/token but entropy low.
findings.impact: Guessed code allows account access without password.

findings.title: Admin actions not logged to immutable audit trail
findings.description: Admin actions logged via console.log to ephemeral CloudWatch without immutability guarantee.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Send logs to immutable store (S3 versioning, audit service). 2. Include actor, action, target, timestamp, IP, result. 3. Set retention 1+ year. 4. Document access controls. 5. Use SIEM for alerting.
findings.evidence: console.log JSON to CloudWatch. No documented retention or immutability.
findings.impact: No tamper-proof audit trail for admin actions.

findings.title: No session invalidation or logout token revocation
findings.description: JWT tokens valid for 30 days with no immediate revocation on logout. Stolen tokens usable until expiry.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Implement token blacklist (DynamoDB). 2. Add JTI (JWT ID) claim. 3. Blacklist on logout. 4. Reduce expiry to 7-14 days.
findings.evidence: Tokens expire after 30 days. No logout revocation mechanism.
findings.impact: Leaked token usable for 30 days.

findings.title: No documented API key rotation policy
findings.description: Secrets in AWS Secrets Manager but no rotation policy or process.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Document rotation frequency/process. 2. Implement Lambda rotation. 3. Create emergency runbook. 4. Log rotations. 5. Test quarterly.
findings.evidence: Secrets Manager loaded but no rotation policy documented.
findings.impact: Compromised secret gives indefinite access.

findings.title: Master encryption key not rotated on password change
findings.description: Master key derived from password; not rotated when password changes. Old keys decrypt historical data.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Re-encrypt journals with new key on password change. 2. Log rotation dates. 3. Offer explicit key rotation. 4. Document process.
findings.evidence: Master key derived from password but not rotated on password change.
findings.impact: Compromised old password still decrypts historical journals.

findings.title: No documented incident response plan
findings.description: No incident response runbook or breach notification procedure documented.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Create incident response runbook. 2. Define roles/escalation. 3. Document 72-hour breach process. 4. Schedule tabletop exercises. 5. Maintain contact list.
findings.evidence: No incident response plan in codebase or CLAUDE.md.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 33-34 and ISO 27001 violations. Cannot meet 72-hour notification.

findings.title: Email regex validation could be stricter
findings.description: Simple regex allows invalid formats, rejects valid ones (e.g., '+' character).
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Use RFC 5322 validation. 2. Allow '+' in email. 3. Consider third-party validation. 4. Test common formats.
findings.impact: Data quality issue; not compliance violation.
